Tony Hooker pedigree (descendant chart for Thomas Hooker)

narrative_blocks.header: Three Hooker brothers, three California fortunes
narrative_blocks.body: Charles Gay arrived in San Francisco in spring 1851 and partnered with younger brother John Delos Hooker at Hooker & Co., 117–119 California Street — the hardware fortune that later funded the 100-inch Hooker Telescope at Mount Wilson. Older brother Henry Clay was simultaneously building Arizona's largest cattle empire.

narrative_blocks.header: Joined the Sons of the American Revolution
narrative_blocks.body: Charles Gay Hooker joined the Sons of the American Revolution, formally documenting his descent from a Revolutionary-era family. He died of pneumonia at age 83 on November 19, 1905, at his home at 967 Bush Street in San Francisco. Buried at Mountain View Cemetery in Oakland — the Bay Area's premier Victorian garden cemetery. (A Findagrave geocoding error lists the death location as 'San Francisco del Mar Municipality, Oaxaca, Mexico'; the actual location is San Francisco, California.)

narrative_blocks.header: A homestead chair followed him to San Francisco
narrative_blocks.body: He took a child's side chair from the Hinsdale homestead with him to San Francisco in 1851. The chair returned east after his 1905 death and now sits at the New Hampshire Historical Society in Concord, provenanced back to his great-grandfather Rev. Bunker Gay.

cross_connections.display_label: Carried his great-grandfather Bunker's Gay surname as a middle name

burial.plot_notes: Mountain View Cemetery, Oakland, Alameda County CA (per Findagrave Memorial 255641260).

careers.employer: Hooker & Co. (with brother John Delos Hooker + Isaac H. Cory)
careers.notes: Came to San Francisco in spring 1851; built fortune in hardware import. Hooker & Co. imported hardware, iron, and related goods. Partnered with brother John Delos Hooker (H02709, who later founded the Hooker Telescope at Mount Wilson Observatory).

Per Truman & Hooker partner biography (re. son Osgood): 'Charles G. Hooker, a prominent hardware merchant of California, who came to this State in 1852, and still resides in San Francisco.' Charles arrived CA 1852 — placed him in the second wave of Gold Rush migrants (1849-1852). Hardware merchandise business connects to the broader CA development boom serving miners + settlers.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Grew up on the Hinsdale homestead before sailing to California in 1851; the family chair he took with him now sits at the New Hampshire Historical Society.

Per Truman & Hooker partner biography (re. son Osgood): 'Charles G. Hooker, a prominent hardware merchant of California, who came to this State in 1852, and still resides in San Francisco.' bio.extra_names: Tony Hooker pedigree records full first name as 'Arrabella' — formal name 'Arrabella Maria Celia Osgood'. EH 1909 lists her simply as 'Maria Celia (Osgood)' (using middle/Latin name in everyday use).

sources.short: Tony Hooker pedigree (descendant chart for Thomas Hooker)

burial.plot_notes: Cypress Lawn Memorial Park, Colma, San Mateo County CA. Same as wife Ella Wall Goad. (per Findagrave)

careers.employer: First National Bank of San Francisco
careers.notes: Per period business biography: 'He was receiving teller in the First National Bank from 1881 till 1885' — entry-level banking position from age 21 to 25.

careers.employer: Truman & Hooker (hay presses, agricultural implements, wagons, buggies)
careers.notes: Joined Irwin J. Truman's existing firm in 1885 at age 25, leaving his First National Bank position. The firm had been founded by Truman in 1867 at 1717 Front Street SF as a manufacturer and dealer in HAY PRESSES; in ~1882 (after 15 years) they added agricultural implements + wagons + buggies. Osgood had charge of the buying and selling departments. Wholesale + retail; trade extended over the Pacific Coast and to the Sandwich Islands [Hawaii], with goods also shipped to Russia and Egypt. ~30 employees. Per period business biography: 'Mr. Hooker is a quick, intelligent and obliging business man, a Californian of whom California has no reason to be ashamed.'

education.notes: Per period business biography: 'educated in the public schools of this city [SF] and at Exeter, New Hampshire.' Phillips Exeter Academy is the prestigious New England preparatory school. The NH connection ties to his father's New Hampshire roots (Charles Gay Hooker b. Hinsdale NH 1820).

memberships.notes: Listed in 1905 Officers & Members directory; address 16 Drumm
Per Bohemian Club Officers & Members 1892 roster: 'HOOKER, G. Osgood' — alongside brother Robert G. Hooker and brother-in-law George H. Lent.

One of SF's most exclusive private clubs. Membership inferred from Sam's earlier note about Robert G. Hooker + Eugene Lent being members; Osgood likely also a member as the elder brother. Specific roster year not yet confirmed.

narrative_blocks.header: Banker at twenty, importer at twenty-five
narrative_blocks.body: From 1881 to 1885 he was receiving teller at the First National Gold Bank of San Francisco. Then he joined Irwin Truman to form Truman and Hooker, importers of agricultural machinery.

narrative_blocks.header: Sold San Francisco wagons to Russia and Egypt
narrative_blocks.body: Truman and Hooker's trade extended across the Pacific to Hawaii, then on to Russia and Egypt. The reach was unusual for an 1890s San Francisco firm.

narrative_blocks.header: Phillips Exeter prepared him from California
narrative_blocks.body: He left San Francisco public schools for the New Hampshire boarding school in his teens. Eighty years later Jay Rockefeller would follow the same path.

narrative_blocks.header: His Bohemian Club fellow married his sister
narrative_blocks.body: In 1892 he and his brother Robert appeared on the Bohemian Club roster alongside George H. Lent. That same year Lent married their sister Bessie Augusta Hooker — bringing the brother-in-law into the family.

cross_connections.display_label: Bohemian Club fellows the year Lent married his sister Bessie.

military_service.notes: Per Findagrave: VVETERAN designation. Era and conflict unspecified — given his birth year 1860, he would have been ~38 at the Spanish-American War (1898) which is the most likely fit. Other possibilities: military service in California state militia. INVESTIGATION FLAG.

burial.plot_notes: Cypress Lawn Memorial Park, Colma, San Mateo County CA. Same as parents and husband Charles Osgood Hooker (per Findagrave).

death.note: Per Tony Hooker pedigree + Sam's data: lived Hillsborough San Mateo County ~10 years before death; lived San Mateo CA in 1939

burial.plot_notes: Cremated at Cypress Lawn Memorial Park Colma but NOT INTERRED there — disposition of cremains uncertain. Per Findagrave: 'Per Cypress Lawn Records, Robert Hooker was cremated at their facility but not interred there. You can call the office at 650-755-0580 to inquire of the disposition of cremains. Perhaps these children of Charles are in the family Mausoleum???'

careers.role: Real estate agent / house broker / rent collector / insurance agent
careers.employer: Hooker & Lent (Robert G. Hooker + George H. Lent)
careers.location: 14 Post Street, San Francisco CA (telephone: Private Exchange 120)
careers.notes: Real estate agency, house brokerage, rent collection, and insurance.

Real estate and investment firm. Founded with brother-in-law George Heydenfeldt Lent (X02298, husband of his sister Bessie H04268) and brother-in-law Eugene Lent. The firm operated through at least 1932 per SF Real Estate Board Yearbook; building referenced in 1916 SF Commercial Club records. Robert G. Hooker was a Pacific-Union Club member (1932 roster) — same as his Lent business partners.
Per 1880s SF directory: 'Hooker Robert G., cashier Am. Sugar Refinery Co., r. 917 Bush' — early-career position before founding Hooker & Lent.

memberships.notes: One of the most exclusive private clubs in the western US. Robert was a member alongside his Lent business partners (his sister Bessie's husband George Heydenfeldt Lent and brother-in-law Eugene Lent).
One of the most exclusive private clubs in the western US. Per SFgenealogy 1932 roster.
Per Bohemian Club Officers & Members 1892 roster: 'HOOKER, Robert G.' — alongside brother G. Osgood Hooker and brother-in-law George H. Lent (married Bessie that same year).

Per Burlingame Country Club roster: 'HOOKER. ROBERT G.. Cunard Bldg.. 503 Market St.. San Francisco. Calif.' — listed alongside several other Hooker family members including 'Lent Hooker' (likely Rodman Lent Hooker HD3246), 'John R. Hooker' (likely John Rodman Hooker HD3243 at Curran & Dwyer), 'Osgood Hooker' (likely Osgood Hooker H05205 — C. Osgood's son), 'Mrs. C. Osgood Hooker' (Ella Wall Goodfellow Hooker, his sister-in-law widow), and 'Robert G. Hooker Jr.' (660 Market Street, Gladding McBean & Co).

narrative_blocks.header: Real estate broker with two brothers-in-law
narrative_blocks.body: He partnered with George and Eugene Lent in the Hooker & Lent firm at 503 Market Street in San Francisco. The firm operated through 1932 and built a long-running family-business network.

narrative_blocks.header: Studied mechanics at Berkeley, ended in real estate
narrative_blocks.body: He took a Ph.B. in Mechanics at the University of California, Berkeley. After early years as cashier at the American Sugar Refining Company, he settled into real estate brokerage.

narrative_blocks.header: Member of San Francisco's three elite clubs
narrative_blocks.body: He belonged to the Bohemian Club from 1892, the Pacific-Union Club, and the Burlingame Country Club. The Hooker family appeared on the Burlingame roster across three generations.

cross_connections.display_label: Brothers-in-law and partners in Hooker & Lent real estate.
Three brothers-in-law ran Hooker & Lent real estate firm.
Bore great-great-grandfather Bunker's Gay name into 1862 San Francisco
was his father-in-law; Founded Shreve & Co.

education.notes: Per Cal Berkeley alumni directory: 'Hooker, Robert Gay, Ph.B. (Mechanics), Real estate, 1117 Pine st, S F'

residences.source: 1880s SF directory (early career, cashier at Am. Sugar Refinery)

sources.short: Tony Hooker pedigree (descendant chart for Thomas Hooker)

cross_connections.display_label: Great-granddaughter Katherine carries her Shreve surname forward
Grandson Anthony carries her Shreve surname forward

bio.extra_names: Named after his great-uncle John Daggett Hooker (H02709), the Hooker Telescope donor

marriages.notes: Per Golden Nugget Library: married Josephine Dunham in 1934. ONE SON: Anthony Shreve Hooker (b.1938, living, NOT built per database living-person rule). Anthony — known as 'Tony' — is Sam Talcott Hooker's first cousin once removed (colloquially 'uncle'). Tony graduated US Naval Academy B.S. 1961 + San Francisco Art Institute M.F.A. 1995; Principal/Portfolio Manager at Ashfield & Co./Ashfield Capital Partners 1986-present. Tony has been a major source for the Hooker family pedigree.

sources.short: Tony Hooker pedigree (descendant chart for Thomas Hooker)
1923 passport #49791 (travel to British Isles, France, Switzerland, Italy, Belgium, Gibraltar for education)
1934-1935 SS Santa Rosa SF→NY passenger list
1940 Census Hillsborough San Mateo Co CA
Golden Nugget Library — Sons of California (online genealogy)

narrative_blocks.header: Named after his telescope-donor great-uncle
narrative_blocks.body: He was named for his father's uncle John Daggett Hooker, who had built the iron-and-oil fortune that funded the 100-inch Hooker Telescope at Mount Wilson. Tony Hooker preserves a photo with his father's inscription: 'uncle of my father, after whom I was named.'

narrative_blocks.header: Harvard A.B. 1925; Hooker & Fay senior partner
narrative_blocks.body: After Harvard A.B. 1925 he became senior partner of Hooker & Fay brokerage at 340 Pine Street San Francisco in 1929 — just before the October crash. He chaired the SF Stock Exchange Finance Committee in 1948-1949.

narrative_blocks.header: Sailed transatlantic with wife Josephine multiple times
narrative_blocks.body: He and his second wife Josephine Dunham made multiple transatlantic voyages between 1934 and 1955, sailing the SS Santa Rosa, the Queen Mary, and others. He lived through Hillsborough, San Mateo, and Sonoma before his 1983 death in Riverside.

narrative_blocks.header: Father of Anthony 'Tony' Shreve Hooker
narrative_blocks.body: His only son Anthony Shreve Hooker, born 1938, became Principal and Portfolio Manager at Ashfield Capital Partners and the primary contemporary documentor of the Hooker pedigree. Tony's middle name carries forward his maternal grandfather Shreve's family — founders of San Francisco's oldest commercial establishment.

cross_connections.display_label: Named in honor of his telescope-funding great-uncle.

education.notes: Per Golden Nugget Library: graduated 1921. Member of Alumni Association; later President of the Board of Trustees of his alma mater. The Santa Barbara School was a college-preparatory boys' school of the era.

Per Golden Nugget Library: A.B. 1925. The 1921 RMS Aquitania voyage to Europe (London → Italy → Switzerland) was Harvard summer or pre-matriculation — NOT a Yale connection as I previously inferred from the family pattern of his Yale-bound great-uncle Laurence Whitney Hooker.

careers.notes: Per Golden Nugget Library: senior partner of Hooker & Fay since 1929. Offices at 340 Pine Street, San Francisco; also 1429 Stockton Street SF; 352 Village Lane Los Gatos CA; and 420 South Main Street Salinas CA — a multi-office brokerage operation across central and northern California.

Per Golden Nugget Library: Chairman of finance committee for years 1948 and 1949. The SF Stock Exchange operated as the Pacific Coast's primary regional exchange before its 1957 merger with the Los Angeles Stock Exchange to form the Pacific Coast Stock Exchange.

careers.employer: The Santa Barbara School Alumni Association
careers.notes: Per Golden Nugget Library: continued involvement with his prep school after 1921 graduation.

memberships.notes: Per Golden Nugget Library. Continues the family pattern: father Robert Gay Hooker (H04266) and great-uncles Charles Gay (H02703) + C. Osgood (H04265) were all PUC members in the 1905 directory.

birth.historical_notes: Per Tony Hooker pedigree + Sam family knowledge: b.1909 — likely Hillsborough or San Francisco given father Robert Gay's residence pattern

marriages.notes: First wife (per WikiTree + 1940 census + family records). Two sons: Michael G. Hooker (Sam Talcott Hooker's father, b.~1936, NOT BUILT per living rule) and Rodman L. Hooker Jr. (HD3315, 1940-2016). Separated post-1940 census; Nancy went on to Walker then Borland. Rodman Lent later remarried.
Native of Pasadena; previously married Thomas Bunn of Pebble Beach (3 Bunn children). Died June 1, 1969 at home 761 Chiltern Rd Hillsborough age 54; coroner-investigated unattended death.
Third wife. Born Harrisburg PA 1925. Three Sollner children from prior marriage. No children with Rodman. Survived him by 14 years; d. Tampa FL Oct 5, 2008 age 83.

sources.short: Direct family information from Sam Talcott Hooker (curator)
sources.page: Sam Hooker confirmed: "Rodman Lent who was my grandfather"
'Grandson of Elizabeth Shreve and Robert Gay Hooker of Hillsborough'

narrative_blocks.header: First name echoes his Shreve grandmother's surname
narrative_blocks.body: His mother Elizabeth Lawton Shreve had carried Rodman as a maternal-line name through her own mother Rebecca Rodman Nichols. His uncle George Rodman Shreve, a partner in the Shreve & Co. SF jewelry firm, also carried it forward.

narrative_blocks.header: Three marriages: Morse, Austin, Sollner
narrative_blocks.body: He married Nancy Morse in July 1934 and had two sons Michael and Rodman Jr. before their post-1940 separation. He later married Jane Elise Austin (d. 1969 in Hillsborough) and Louise Henrichsen Sollner (d. 2008 in Tampa).

cross_connections.display_label: Middle name 'Lent' honors his father's business partner.

birth.historical_notes: Born to Robert Gay Hooker Sr. + Elizabeth Lawton Shreve, likely SF or Hillsborough San Mateo County

sources.short: Tony Hooker pedigree (Robert Gay Hooker Sr. children)
sources.page: Robert Gay Hooker Jr. b.1901 d.1984

narrative_blocks.header: Managed Harvard's freshman hockey team in 1920
narrative_blocks.body: He was appointed manager of the Harvard freshman hockey team on February 11, 1920, with classmate Donald Fairfax Bush Jr. as assistant. He graduated Harvard in the class of 1923 from Burlingame, California.

narrative_blocks.header: Married daughter of American Water Works founder
narrative_blocks.body: He married Marion Sabina Kuhn (1904-2000), daughter of Pittsburgh-born industrialist William Speer Kuhn — founder of the American Water Works Company. The marriage joined two transcontinental industrial fortunes.

burial.plot_notes: Cypress Lawn Memorial Park, Colma San Mateo County CA

birth.historical_notes: DATE DISCREPANCY: Findagrave records 3 Dec 1868. FamilySearch records 31 Dec 1868. Going with Findagrave (cemetery primary source). Father Charles Gay Hooker (H02703) was 48; mother Maria Celia Osgood (X02249) was 32. State: California.

marriages.notes: George Heydenfeldt Solomon Lent (1868-1942) — same year birth/death as Bessie. Business partner with Bessie's brother Robert Gay Hooker (H04266) in Hooker & Lent / Lent & Hooker real estate (14 Post Street SF + Mills Building). Bessie's nephew Rodman Lent Hooker (HD3246, Sam Talcott Hooker's grandfather) was named for the Lent partnership.

sources.short: California Academy of Sciences Archives — Alice Eastwood Correspondents

narrative_blocks.header: Married into her brother's business partner
narrative_blocks.body: Bessie Augusta Hooker — daughter of Charles Gay Hooker and Maria Celia Osgood — married George Heydenfeldt Solomon Lent on October 8, 1892 in San Francisco. George (1868-1942) was the business partner of Bessie's brother Robert Gay Hooker in the firm Hooker & Lent (later Lent & Hooker), real estate, with offices at 14 Post Street SF and the Mills Building. The marriage drew the two families' commercial fortunes still closer.

narrative_blocks.header: Hooker line continues to modern Howard cousins
narrative_blocks.body: Through Bessie Augusta and George H. Lent the Hooker line continues into a separate branch of modern California cousins. Their only child Elizabeth Lent (, b.1914) married Dr. Lot D.

narrative_blocks.header: Wrote condolence letter to grieving Jane Stanford
narrative_blocks.body: In 1884 — when Bessie Augusta was 16 — Leland Stanford Jr. died of typhoid fever in Florence at age 15, the only son of California's railroad king and former governor Leland Stanford Sr. and his wife Jane. Bessie Augusta Hooker (later Mrs. George H. Lent) wrote a personal condolence letter to Jane Stanford, signing 'Bessie Hooker Lent' from her San Francisco address at 2229 Washington Street: 'Words at such a time, I know, seem cold and meaningless and bring but little comfort, but I felt I could but tell you, after your great kindness to me, how much we sympathize and thought of you in your great sorrow.' The letter is preserved in the Stanford University Archives among the condolence letters re: the death of Leland Stanford Jr. The Hookers and Stanfords moved in overlapping San Francisco mercantile circles; Leland Jr.'s memory two years later (1886) became the foundation of Stanford University.

narrative_blocks.header: Corresponded with botanist Alice Eastwood, 1926
narrative_blocks.body: In 1926 Bessie Hooker Lent appears in the California Academy of Sciences Archives as a correspondent of Alice Eastwood — the legendary botanist and Director of Botany at the Academy from 1894-1949 (with her famous 1906 rescue of 1,500 type specimens during the San Francisco earthquake fire). The single dated entry from that year places Bessie among Eastwood's network of botanical correspondents alongside the Royal Botanic Gardens at Kew, the Imperial Botanical Garden in St. Petersburg, and Huntington Gardens. Bessie's interest in botany echoes the Hooker family's long scientific tradition — though no relation, the family shared the surname with Sir William Jackson Hooker (founding Director of Kew) and Sir Joseph Dalton Hooker.

cross_connections.evidence: Bessie's husband George H. Lent partnered with her brother Robert Gay Hooker (H04266) in Hooker & Lent real estate, San Francisco.
cross_connections.display_label: Married her brother's real-estate business partner
was her father-in-law; California '49er pioneer; SF businessman

residences.location: Judicial Township 3, San Mateo County, California, USA

burial.plot_notes: Cypress Lawn Memorial Park, Colma San Mateo Co CA. Cremated.

bio.extra_names: FamilySearch records spelling variant 'Haydenfelt' (without 'e'); Lent family book + Wikipedia spelling 'Heydenfeldt' is canonical via the Solomon Heydenfeldt namesake.

birth.historical_notes: Per Lent family book entry #78: b. January 23, 1868. Likely San Francisco given his father William M. Lent's SF residence.

death.note: Cremated at Cypress Lawn Memorial Park Colma per Findagrave + cemetery records.

sources.short: James Arthur Lent, History of the Lent (van Lent) Family in America (1902)
FamilySearch: George Haydenfelt Lent / Bessie Augusta Hooker

narrative_blocks.header: Co-founded Hooker & Lent SF real estate firm
narrative_blocks.body: His 'Hooker & Lent' San Francisco real estate firm at 503 Market Street operated from the 1890s through at least 1932. Partners were his brother-in-law Robert G Hooker (Bessie's brother) and his own brother Eugene Lent — a family business joining the Lent and Hooker SF dynasties.

narrative_blocks.header: Three Hooker men already Bohemian Club fellows
narrative_blocks.body: He joined the Bohemian Club in 1892 — alongside Bessie's brothers G Osgood Hooker and Robert G Hooker, who were already fellows the year of his October wedding. The three Hooker family men formed the anchor of the social circle George married into.

narrative_blocks.header: Predeceased wife Bessie by five months, both 1942
narrative_blocks.body: He died 9 March 1942 in San Francisco at seventy-four. His wife Bessie Augusta Hooker died five months later on 19 August 1942 — both cremated at Cypress Lawn Memorial Park Colma.

narrative_blocks.header: Likely named for Justice Solomon Heydenfeldt
narrative_blocks.body: His middle name almost certainly honors Solomon Heydenfeldt (1816-1890), Charleston-born Jewish jurist who served as California Supreme Court Justice 1852-1857. Heydenfeldt was prominent in San Francisco where George's father William M. Lent lived.

cross_connections.display_label: Married Robert's sister Bessie; partners in Hooker & Lent firm.
Bohemian Club fellows the year he married Hooker's sister.
Robert Gay's son carries 'Lent' as his middle name.
Robert Gay's grandson carries 'Lent' as his middle name.

careers.notes: Per his father William Mandeville Lent's October 1904 SF Call obituary: 'George H. Lent of the real estate firm of Hooker & Lent.' The firm was a major SF real estate and investment business at 503 Market Street. Partners with Robert G. Hooker (H04266) — Bessie's brother — and brother Eugene Lent (X-prefix above). The firm operated through at least 1932 per the SF Real Estate Board Yearbook.

X02298.siblings_research: Eldest known siblings (per Lent book #48): William H. Lent (#75, b. Mar 16, 1858 — d. without issue), Fanny A. Lent (#76, b. Mar 18, 1862 — never married), Eugene Lent (#77, b. Sep 12, 1863 — X-prefix above)

burial.plot_notes: Cypress Lawn Memorial Park, Colma San Mateo Co CA. Cremated.

memberships.notes: Per Bohemian Club Officers & Members 1892 roster: 'LENT, George H.' Listed alongside brothers-in-law G. Osgood Hooker and Robert G. Hooker — three Hooker family members in the Bohemian Club the same year George married Bessie.

bio.bio_blurb: Only child bridging the Hooker-Lent and Howard lines

birth.historical_notes: Per Sam family knowledge: only child of Bessie Augusta Hooker (H04268) and George H. Lent. Born 1914 — likely SF given parents' SF residence.

death.note: Deceased per Tony Hooker pedigree ("1914-Deceased"); specific year not yet documented in available sources. Predeceased her son Lent Howard (d.2023) per his obituary which lists her as "predeceased by his mother."

marriages.notes: At least 4 children: TWIN SONS Duncan Lent Howard (HD3250, b.1940 LIVING — previously misreported as deceased 2012, that was wrong) and George Lent Howard (HD3251, 1940-2012); plus daughters Pamela Howard (m. Castle) and Tracey Howard Pullen (m. Pullen) — both daughters intentionally not built (status uncertain).

sources.short: Tony Hooker pedigree (Bessie Augusta + George Lent line)
George Lent Howard obituary (Marin Independent Journal, Nov 2012) — Elizabeth's son

narrative_blocks.header: Bridge from Hooker line to modern Howard cousins
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth Lent (b.1914) was the only child of Bessie Augusta Hooker and George Heydenfeldt Solomon Lent (her brother-in-law to Robert Gay Hooker through the Hooker-Lent real estate partnership). She married Dr. Lot D. Howard Jr., son of Lot Duncan Howard Sr. — a Stanford-trained zoologist who authored two anatomy textbooks at Stanford University Press in 1928-29 with Edwin Chapin Starks: 'The Dissection of the Turtle' (1928) and 'The Dissection of the Toad' (1929).

narrative_blocks.header: Twin sons born the same September day
narrative_blocks.body: Bessie Hooker’s only child married Dr. Lot Howard Jr. and bore twin sons in San Francisco on September 9, 1940 — Duncan and George Lent Howard — plus daughters Pamela and Tracey, carrying the Hooker-Lent line into the modern Howard cousins.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Inherited the Hinsdale homestead from his father Seth; died in the house where he had been born ninety-three years earlier.

career.notes: Learned the trade in early life; pursued carriage making while also farming the homestead.
Worked the homestead through old age; gave full attention to farming after abandoning the carriage trade.

narrative_blocks.header: His sons launched three California empires
narrative_blocks.body: Six of his eleven children left Hinsdale for the West. Charles Gay built the San Francisco hardware fortune, Henry Clay founded Arizona's first cattle ranch, and John Daggett funded the 100-inch Hooker Telescope at Mount Wilson.

narrative_blocks.header: Ninety-three years in one Hinsdale farmhouse
narrative_blocks.body: Born July 1791 in the room overlooking the Gay homestead and dying in March 1885, he passed his entire life on one farm in Hinsdale. The Gazetteer of Cheshire County called him 'a hale and hearty old man.'

narrative_blocks.header: Crossed to California twice in his eighties
narrative_blocks.body: After eighty he made two trips out to visit his California sons, riding the railroads across a continent he had been born too early to see imagined. He returned each time to the Hinsdale homestead.

parents.father_research_notes: Simeon Daggett (1758-1819). Per WikiTree (cited in Sam's research). Likely descended from the Massachusetts colonial Daggett line (John Daggett of Watertown MA, immigrant ~1630s).
parents.mother_research_notes: Anna Dean (1758-c.1819). The matching birth/death dates with Simeon (per WikiTree) are likely a data error in the WikiTree record — almost certainly Anna had different death date than Simeon. Maiden name Dean.

narrative_blocks.header: Mother of Henry Clay Hooker, Arizona cattle baron
narrative_blocks.body: She raised eleven children at Hinsdale on the Connecticut River. Her son Henry Clay Hooker founded the Sierra Bonita Ranch in Arizona Territory.

narrative_blocks.header: Two Daggetts married into the Hooker line
narrative_blocks.body: Her 1816 marriage to Henry Hooker came four years after Elisabeth Daggett, in a separate Hooker branch, married Edward Hooker. The two Daggett brides may share earlier Massachusetts ancestry.

X02247.siblings_research: Per WikiTree: 8 documented Daggett siblings: Simeon Jr (M, 1780-1854), Sarah M. (F, 1783-?), (unnamed son b.1784-?), Nathaniel (M, 1785-1826), Nancy (F, 1788-1854), Caleb C (M, 1792-1879), Silvey (F, 1792-1883 — possibly twin of Caleb), and Mary herself (1795-1864). Mary was the youngest of 8.

classification.lineage_note: H00526 Seth (gen 5) → H00264 Rev. John (gen 4) → H00098 John No.2 (gen 3) → H00030 John (gen 2) → H00007 Samuel (gen 1) → H00001 Thomas

compiler_connection.notes: On the compiler's paternal (Hooker surname) line. Marriage to Abigail Gay explains the middle name 'Gay' in the compiler's father Michael Gay Hooker. The Gay family farm at Hinsdale NH became the Hooker place.
compiler_connection.path_to_compiler: Seth(H00526) → [descendants to trace] → Michael Gay Hooker → Samuel Talcott Hooker

open_questions.question: Father Rev. John Hooker of Northampton — EH entry number
open_questions.notes: Son of Rev. John and Sarah (Worthington) Hooker of Northampton, MA. Rev. John descends through H00098 (John No. 2, b. 1695). Need EH entry number.
open_questions.action: Look up Rev. John Hooker of Northampton in EH index

military_service.notes: EH: 'though very young he had some service' — born 1759, so approximately age 15-18 during service

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Came to the Hinsdale homestead in 1789 through his marriage to Abigail Gay; worked the farm and made hats there for fifty-five years.

narrative_blocks.header: Marriage carried Hookers north to Hinsdale, 1789
narrative_blocks.body: Born and raised at Northampton, he served briefly in the Revolution as a teenager and learned hatmaking. His 1789 marriage to Abigail Gay of Hinsdale brought him onto her father Bunker's homestead, establishing the Hooker family on what became their New Hampshire seat for four generations.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Born and raised on her father Rev. Bunker Gay's homestead; her 1789 marriage to Seth Hooker brought him onto the farm.

INST094.name: Pacific-Union Club

founding_year.founder: Merger of Pacific Club (founded 1852) and Union Club (founded 1854)

location.address: 1000 California Street (Flood Mansion atop Nob Hill since 1912)

INST094.description: The Pacific-Union Club is San Francisco's oldest and most exclusive private men's club, formed in 1889 by the merger of the Pacific Club (1852) and the Union Club (1854) — both gold-rush-era gentlemen's clubs. Since 1912 it has occupied the James C. Flood Mansion atop Nob Hill at 1000 California Street, the only Nob Hill mansion to survive the 1906 earthquake and fire. Membership has historically included San Francisco's mercantile, banking, legal, and political elite. The Hooker family carried multi-generational membership: the 1905 Officers and Members directory lists three Hooker descendants — Charles Gay Hooker (H02703, address 967 Bush), C. Osgood Hooker (H04265, his son), and R.G. Hooker (Robert Gay Hooker, H04266, address 14 Post). A generation later, Charles Gay's grandson John Rodman Hooker (HD3243, Hooker & Fay senior partner) was also a member per his Golden Nugget Library biography. The Hooker presence at the club thus spans four generations — and is presumed to have continued with John Rodman's son Anthony Shreve 'Tony' Hooker.
INST094.notes:
[merged from INST100]: Founded 1889 by merger of two earlier SF clubs (Pacific Club + Union Club). Houses in the historic James C. Flood Mansion atop Nob Hill — one of the few buildings to survive the 1906 earthquake. One of the most exclusive private clubs in the western US. Hooker family members documented: Eugene Lent (X02301, 1924+1932 rosters per SFgenealogy), Robert G. Hooker (H04266, 1932 roster). C. Osgood Hooker (H04265) likely also a member but specific roster confirmation pending.
[type detail: category=social_club]
[type detail: sub_category=private_club]
[research_notes] Three Hooker descendants documented as 1905 members: father Charles Gay (H02703), son C. Osgood (H04265), and son Robert Gay (H04266). The Hooker mercantile dynasty was an integral part of late-19th and early-20th-century SF business society. The Flood Mansion home of the club is itself a survivor of the 1906 earthquake — one of very few Nob Hill structures still standing — making the club a physical link to gold-rush-era San Francisco.

crossConnections.display_label: Carried his great-grandfather Bunker's Gay surname as a middle name
